cards.text: A {{c1::constexpr function}} is allowed to be called in a constant expression, while a {{c2::consteval function}} must evaluate at compile-time.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.front: What three compile-time related keywords were covered in Chapter F?
cards.back:
• constexpr - function can evaluate at compile-time or runtime
  • consteval - function must evaluate at compile-time
  • constinit - variable initialized at compile-time but mutable
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.question: In the quiz example, why can't getTowerHeight() be made constexpr?
cards.answer: Because it performs I/O operations (std::cout and std::cin), which can only be done at runtime.
cards.explanation: The I/O operators << and >> for std::cout and std::cin don't support constexpr. Input/output must happen at runtime, not compile-time.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.text: In the quiz example, gravity should be {{c1::constexpr}} because it's a {{c2::compile-time constant}}, but towerHeight can only be {{c3::const}} because its value comes from {{c4::runtime input}}.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.front: In the quiz, why can calculateBallHeight() be made constexpr even though it's called with runtime values?
cards.back: Because constexpr functions can evaluate at either compile-time OR runtime. When called with runtime arguments, it evaluates at runtime like a normal function.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.question: In the quiz, why can't the ballHeight variable be constexpr?
cards.answer: Because its initializer (calculateBallHeight with runtime arguments) is not a constant expression.
cards.explanation: Even though calculateBallHeight() is constexpr, it's being called with towerHeight which has a runtime value. The result is therefore a runtime value, not a compile-time constant.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.text: Function parameters are {{c1::never constexpr}}, even in a {{c2::constexpr function}}.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.concept: Decision tree: const vs constexpr for variables
cards.explanation:
Use this decision tree for variables:
  • Value known at compile-time and won't change? Use constexpr
  • Value from runtime source but won't change after init? Use const
  • Value needs to change? Use neither
Special case: constinit for mutable global/static variables initialized at compile-time.
examples: const double height = getHeight();  // runtime constant
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.front: What is the best practice regarding when to make functions constexpr?
cards.back: If a function CAN be made constexpr (it performs calculations without I/O or other runtime-only operations), it SHOULD be made constexpr.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz

cards.text: Constexpr functions are implicitly {{c1::inline}}, which means they can be {{c2::defined in header files}} without violating the one definition rule.
cards.source: F.X - Chapter F summary and quiz
